results.title: Crunchyroll is DESTROYING Their Subtitles!! Here's Why! (And How To Stop Them)
results.content: were no longer able to overlap text. Signs and dialogue had to share the same space, either at the top or the bottom of the screen, but never both. And why would they do that? Well, Funimation just got a sub-licensing deal with Hulu, and this new subtitle style just so happens to fit into their limited subtitle standards. Where have we heard that before? That's not the only possible reason though, because around the same time, Funimation switched to a new cloud-based subtitling toolkit called Una, purely to make investors cream their jeans. Una is a very limited program and does not natively allow for overlapping subtitles. You have to fuss with the settings a bit, and even then it won't necessarily work for all formats. If you're a company that doesn't exactly care about the quality of [...] They now have to pay people to convert every anime they want to sub-license from Crunchyroll's superior subtitle format to that service's terrible subtitle standards. But what if there was another way? There are two easy ways to reduce labor costs. One, remove the labor entirely, or two, replace the laborer with a machine. That second one is foreshadowing. Crunchyroll realized that if they change their subtitling standards to match the other streaming services, there would be no need to convert them and they could cut out a lot of labor. And that's exactly what they've started to do. But even in that context, this still seems like a monumentally stupid idea. Best viewed on Crunchyroll should be a pretty damn good way to maintain long-term growth while still getting the money from these [...] reason by Dies. It's a fantastic article. Definitely check it out in the description below for more info and links on how to help. It gets way more into the weeds on the interesting technical details of subtitling than I'm going to here, mainly because I'm worried about messing something up in the paraphrasing. I'm making this to help spread the word so we can work together to get Crunchyroll to stop this change before it's too late. If you care about anime at all, I hope you'll join me. Chapter 1, what's the problem? Crunchyroll has some of the best subtitles in the anime distribution industry currently. Unlike Amazon and Netflix, their major competitors in the space, Crunchyroll subtitles have the ability to keep pace with dialogue while also presenting translations of on-screen text

Mark, Breakthrough with Rod Parsley

### Contact info [...] Under the new rules, viewers who have a complaint can contact the station where the problem was seen, or contact the FCC, which will forward the report to the broadcaster, cable company, or satellite provider.

### Timely Resolution of Closed Captioning Complaints

Simple problems can be fixed within minutes, if consumers call or e-mail right away.

Some people may decide to contact broadcasters later, but the new rules state that complaints must be made within 60 days of the captioning problem.

Then the program producer has 30 days to respond to the complaint.

## How To Contact The FCC:

### Online

File your complaint using the FCC Complaint Form.

### Email

File your complaint by email to (/cdn-cgi/l/email-protection)

### Fax

1-866-418-0232

### Letter

results.title: Why is Closed Captioning Not Working? Fix Subtitle Errors Fast (2026)
results.content:
### How do I tell if a video even supports subtitles

Look for the "CC" logo on the video's description page. If that icon isn't there, the video likely doesn't have a subtitle track available, and no amount of settings-toggling will make them appear.

 What You Need to Know About General Transcription [...] Try exporting a small segment of the video first or checking your app version. Often, a quick update to the latest version of CapCut resolves bugs with the captioning tool.

## What should I know about captions in other places

Captions aren't always just text on a screen; sometimes the format changes based on where you are.

### What does "Closed Caption" actually mean at a movie theater

In a theater, "Closed" means the captions are only visible to the person who needs them. You are usually given a small device that fits in your cup holder or a pair of glasses that project the text.
